findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: incident date stated as March 14, 2021
findings.description: The location and commercial-site framing are corroborated, but the date is not. The brief says March 14, 2021, while all source documents that date the incident say March 12, 2021.

evidence.quote: This action arises from a workplace incident on March 14, 2021, at a commercial construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Location:          2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, CA 90006
the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The contradiction is mechanically checkable: the motion says March 14, while the medical records, police report, and witness statement all say March 12. This is the same underlying wrong-incident-date defect kept more completely in finding-27.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: incident date stated as March 14, 2021
findings.description: The sources corroborate that Rivera was working on scaffolding when it gave way, but they contradict the brief's date. The brief says March 14, 2021; the medical, police, and witness records all place the incident on March 12, 2021.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 3

evidence.quote: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Patient was working on scaffolding at a commercial construction site when the structure gave way.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021

THE INCIDENT

At approximately 10:45 AM, I was working on a scaffolding section adjacent to where Carlos was positioned, roughly 20 feet to his west.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The date conflict is direct and repeated: the motion uses March 14, while the medical and police records give March 12. This duplicates the same underlying wrong-incident-date defect preserved in finding-27.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: Rivera was not wearing required PPE
findings.description: The brief says Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment. The police report and witness statement say the opposite: he was wearing a hard hat, harness, and high-visibility vest, and his lanyard detached because the anchor point collapsed.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 4

evidence.quote: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse.
The harness lanyard was found still attached to Rivera but had pulled free from the scaffolding anchor point when the structure gave way.
Carlos was wearing his hard hat, safety harness, and high-visibility vest, consistent with site PPE requirements.
His harness was still on, but the lanyard had come free â€” the anchor point it was attached to was part of the scaffolding that collapsed.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The contradiction is explicit: the motion says Rivera lacked required PPE, while both the police report and witness statement say he wore a hard hat and harness. This is material because PPE use bears directly on comparative fault and causation.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: incident date stated as March 14, 2021
findings.description: The brief states the incident occurred on March 14, 2021. Every source document that gives the incident date states March 12, 2021, making the brief's date incompatible with the record.

evidence.quote: The incident giving rise to this action occurred on March 14, 2021.
DATE OF ADMISSION:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: This is mechanically checkable: the motion says March 14, while every supplied record that dates the incident says March 12. It is kept as the lead duplicate because it contains the broadest date contradiction and appears in the limitations section where the date is legally consequential.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Privette v. Superior Court appears doctored
findings.description: Privette is a real, well-known California Supreme Court case, but the quoted sentence uses the absolute phrase “never liable,” which materially overstates the holding. Privette addressed peculiar-risk/vicarious liability and became a general presumption of nonliability subject to recognized exceptions or direct-liability theories in the Privette line, such as retained control that affirmatively contributes to injury, concealed hazards, or furnishing defective equipment. Known actual language/rule: Privette held that the peculiar-risk doctrine generally does not allow an independent contractor's employee, whose injury is covered by workers' compensation, to recover tort damages from the hirer on a vicarious-liability theory. It did not abolish all possible hirer liability for such injuries.

evidence.quote: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: Privette's nonliability rule and its exceptions are canonical California doctrine, and the quoted absolute phrase “never liable” is materially inconsistent with that doctrine. Confidence is capped because the actual Privette text is not supplied in the evidence packet.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: exclusive Apex responsibility for scaffolding and safety
findings.description: The sources support that Apex employed Rivera, but they undercut the exclusive framing that only Apex was responsible for the scaffolding work and safety. Harmon foreman Ray Donner directed the crew where to work and, according to Tran, dismissed a specific base-plate safety concern before the collapse.
findings.brief_location: III.A. Harmon Is Not Liable Under the Privette Doctrine

evidence.quote: Apex Staffing Solutions â€” not Harmon â€” was the employer responsible for scaffolding operations and for ensuring its employees followed appropriate safety procedures.
Donner stated that he had directed Rivera and his crew to begin work on the east-side scaffolding section earlier that morning.
When asked whether he had inspected the scaffolding section before directing the crew to use it, Donner stated, "That's Apex's responsibility. I told them where we needed them working."
During our morning coordination meeting, Ray Donner â€” the project foreman for Harmon Construction â€” told us that the east-side scaffolding section needed to be fully operational by end of day.
Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
I want to note that Donner had personally directed us to work on that section that morning and dismissed my concern about the base plate.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence strongly undercuts the motion's exclusive “Apex — not Harmon” framing because Donner allegedly directed the work and dismissed a base-plate safety concern. Some interpretation is required because Apex could still have had substantial responsibility despite Harmon's involvement.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
findings.description: I do not recognize this case. There are concrete fabrication signals: 887 F.2d is inconsistent with a 1991 Ninth Circuit citation as I know the F.2d volume chronology, and the quoted OSHA-compliance 'rebuttable presumption' rule does not match a familiar California negligence rule. The case name is also generic and construction-themed in a way that fits the brief suspiciously well. Jurisdiction concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California tort-law standards of care.

evidence.quote: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991) ("Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.").

findings.confidence_reasoning: The F.2d volume/date mismatch and unfamiliar OSHA-presumption rule are concrete warning signs, but there is no supplied reporter/database record confirming nonexistence. Confidence is limited because the finding still relies on legal/citation memory rather than mechanically checked sources.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co.
findings.description: I do not recognize this C.D. Cal. case, and there are concrete fabrication signals: the case name and parenthetical are unusually tailored to the brief's exact scaffolding/contractor facts, and the parenthetical's 'assumed risks inherent to the trade' framing sounds suspiciously crafted rather than like a known Privette holding. The reporter citation is facially plausible, but I cannot confirm it from memory. Jurisdiction concern: A C.D. Cal. decision would be at most persuasive, not binding, in a California Superior Court on California tort law.

evidence.quote: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004) (granting summary judgment to hirer where subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade).

findings.confidence_reasoning: The only supplied evidence is the brief's citation, and the finding depends largely on non-recognition and suspicious tailoring rather than a database hit or contrary reporter entry. It is kept because fabricated authority would be serious and the parenthetical is unusually fact-matched, but verification is weak.

findings.title: Time-barred heading contradicted by conceded nominally timely filing
findings.description: The section heading asserts the claims are time-barred, but the body concedes the filing falls within this window under the statute of limitations (CCP 335.1 / two years = 730 days). Filing on 2023-03-10 is 726 days after the incident on 2021-03-14, which is inside the 730-day window. The time-barred framing is therefore self-defeating.

evidence.quote: D. Rivera's Claims Are Time-Barred

While Rivera's filing falls nominally within this window, Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The internal tension is apparent from the quoted heading and the quoted concession that the filing falls nominally within the limitations window. Confidence is slightly limited because the conclusion also relies on the brief's own filing date and incident date assumptions, and the body preserves an accrual argument.

findings.title: Legal argument advanced without supporting authority
findings.description: The brief states and applies rules of law without citing any case, statute, or regulation in support. Uncited assertions: (1) at III.C, paragraph 2: Under the doctrine of assumption of risk, a plaintiff who voluntarily encounters a known danger cannot recover for injuries arising from that danger. (2) at III.D, paragraph 3: Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

evidence.quote: Under the doctrine of assumption of risk, a plaintiff who voluntarily encounters a known danger cannot recover for injuries arising from that danger.
Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted passages are legal/factual propositions and the finding rests on the CitationExtractor's assessment that no authority accompanied them. Confidence is not higher because the evidence excerpts do not show the surrounding citation context or the full brief section.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
findings.description: The supplied case file includes evidence from Tran and the police report about unsafe scaffolding conditions and pre-incident notice to Harmon foreman Donner. That conflicts with the absolute assertion that Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut reasonable-care or safety-compliance framing.
findings.brief_location: III.B. OSHA Compliance Establishes a Presumption of Reasonable Care

evidence.quote: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.
The base plates on the failed section appeared to be resting on uneven ground, with one plate sitting on a loose plywood sheet rather than a stable footing.
Officers observed that portions of the scaffolding in the affected section showed visible rust and wear.
Tran stated that these concerns were communicated to both Ellison and Donner.
I also mentioned the base plate issue directly to Ray Donner.
The scaffolding he was on was not in safe condition, and concerns about it had been raised before the collapse.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The police report and witness statement contain evidence of unsafe scaffolding and notice to Harmon, which conflicts with an absolute “no evidence” assertion. Confidence is not higher because the procedural phrase “has presented” may refer to the litigation record, not merely documents in the supplied file.

findings.title: Claim asserted without record support: Rivera filed the instant action on March 10, 2023.
findings.description: No complaint, docket sheet, filing stamp, or other procedural filing document is included in the sources. The source documents therefore provide no basis for this filing-date claim.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 6

evidence.quote: Rivera filed the instant action on March 10, 2023.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted filing-date assertion would normally be supported by a complaint, docket, or filing stamp, and the finding says none is in the supplied sources. It duplicates the same unsupported March 10, 2023 filing-date defect preserved in finding-28.

findings.title: Claim asserted without record support: over eight years of commercial-construction experience
findings.description: The source documents identify Rivera as a journeyman scaffolder but do not state his years of experience or that he had over eight years in commercial construction. This is a record-checkable qualification/tenure claim with no support in the supplied documents.
findings.brief_location: III.C. Rivera Assumed the Risk Inherent in His Trade

evidence.quote: with over eight years of experience in commercial construction.

findings.confidence_reasoning: Years of experience is a concrete factual assertion, and the finding says the supplied documents identify Rivera's role but not his tenure. Confidence is limited by the possibility that the omitted support exists outside the provided sources.

findings.title: Claim asserted without record support: Rivera did not file his complaint until March 10, 2023
findings.description: The supplied sources do not include the complaint, docket, filing stamp, or other court record showing when the complaint was filed. No source document supports the March 10, 2023 filing date.

evidence.quote: Rivera did not file his complaint until March 10, 2023

findings.confidence_reasoning: The filing date is a record-checkable procedural fact, and the supplied evidence contains only the motion's assertion with no docket or complaint support. Confidence is limited because the finding establishes lack of support in the provided sources, not that the date is false.

findings.title: Claim asserted without record support: Apex was a licensed subcontractor retained by Harmon
findings.description: The sources support that Apex personnel performed scaffolding work and that Ellison was a subcontractor scaffolding crew lead, but they do not state Apex was licensed or retained by Harmon under a subcontract. No contract, license record, or retention document is included.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 2

evidence.quote: Apex Staffing Solutions, a licensed subcontractor retained by Harmon for scaffolding assembly and related work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted assertion requires licensing and retention/subcontract proof, and the finding states that no such document appears in the supplied sources. Confidence is limited because absence from the provided case file does not prove the fact is false, only unsupported in this record.

findings.title: Authority does not support the stated proposition: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc.
findings.description: SeaBright supports the Privette/delegation doctrine, not the proposition that compliance with statutory safety requirements is 'highly probative' of due care or that an OSHA inspection record insulates a hirer from tort liability. The brief appears to convert SeaBright's delegation holding into an evidentiary compliance/due-care rule, which is not the holding I know.

evidence.quote: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011).

findings.confidence_reasoning: SeaBright is a known Privette-line delegation case, which supports the core critique, but the excerpted evidence contains only the citation and not the proposition or parenthetical being challenged. The severity is reduced because the defect is material but the record provided here does not show that it alone would determine the motion.

findings.title: Brief claim contradicted by case file: elapsed time stated as one year and 362 days
findings.description: Using the source-supported incident date of March 12, 2021 and the brief's asserted filing date of March 10, 2023, the elapsed time is 728 days: 365 days to March 12, 2022 plus 363 days to March 10, 2023. That equals one year and 363 days, not one year and 362 days; using the brief's unsupported March 14 incident date would also not yield one year and 362 days.

evidence.quote: one year and 362 days after the incident.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Patient was brought in by LAFD paramedics following a workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The arithmetic is mechanically checkable from the stated dates: March 12, 2021 to March 10, 2023 is 728 days, not the elapsed period stated in the motion. Severity is reduced because the one-day count error is technical unless tied to a dispositive limitations deadline.

judicial_memo: The brief contains several material defects, including record contradictions on central facts and questionable legal support. Most significantly, it repeatedly states that the incident occurred on March 14, 2021, in the Introduction, Statement of Facts, and limitations section, while every source document that dates the incident places it on March 12, 2021. It also states that Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment, but the police report and witness statement say he was wearing a hard hat, harness, and high-visibility vest, and that his lanyard detached when the anchor point collapsed. The brief’s Privette quotation appears to overstate the rule by using “never liable,” although Privette did not abolish all hirer liability. The record also undercuts the brief’s assertion that only Apex controlled scaffolding safety, as sources describe Harmon foreman Donner directing work and dismissing a base-plate concern. Two cited authorities could not be located in available review, and several factual assertions lack supporting source documents.

citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that under California law, a hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work.

citations.quoted_text: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that the Supreme Court held that a hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

citations.full_citation: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004) (granting summary judgment to hirer where subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade).
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that summary judgment is appropriate for a hirer when the subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade.

citations.full_citation: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991) ("Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.").

citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that full compliance with applicable OSHA standards entitles the employer to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

citations.full_citation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 335.1
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that under California Code of Civil Procedure Section 335.1, the statute of limitations for personal injury claims is two years.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I recognize Privette v. Superior Court as a leading California Supreme Court decision reported at 5 Cal.4th 689 in 1993; the reporter, court, and year are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette held that a person who hires an independent contractor generally is not liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries to the contractor's employees, in significant part because workers' compensation covers the employee and the contractor controls the work. Later California cases describe this as the Privette presumption, subject to recognized exceptions.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition is framed as a presumption of nonliability, which is consistent with the Privette doctrine. The brief should not omit that the presumption is not absolute: exceptions developed in cases such as Hooker v. Department of Transportation, McKown v. Wal-Mart Stores, Inc., and Kinsman v. Unocal Corp. can impose liability where the hirer retained control and affirmatively contributed to the injury, furnished unsafe equipment, or concealed certain hazards.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: Same case as cite-1: Privette v. Superior Court, 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993), is a real and well-known California Supreme Court case.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette adopted a general rule that a hirer is not liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries to an independent contractor's employees arising from the contracted work, but the doctrine is a presumption and later cases recognize exceptions.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The quoted statement that a hirer is 'never liable' is materially overbroad and not a fair statement of California law. Privette does not establish absolute immunity; later controlling California Supreme Court authority recognizes exceptions, including retained control with affirmative contribution, provision of unsafe equipment, and concealed hazards.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this C.D. Cal. case, and there are concrete fabrication signals: the case name and parenthetical are unusually tailored to the brief's exact scaffolding/contractor facts, and the parenthetical's 'assumed risks inherent to the trade' framing sounds suspiciously crafted rather than like a known Privette holding. The reporter citation is facially plausible, but I cannot confirm it from memory.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify the case and see fabrication signals, I cannot assess whether it supports the asserted proposition.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: A C.D. Cal. decision would be at most persuasive, not binding, in a California Superior Court on California tort law.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this case. There are concrete fabrication signals: 887 F.2d is inconsistent with a 1991 Ninth Circuit citation as I know the F.2d volume chronology, and the quoted OSHA-compliance 'rebuttable presumption' rule does not match a familiar California negligence rule. The case name is also generic and construction-themed in a way that fits the brief suspiciously well.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because the citation appears likely fabricated, I cannot evaluate support. Substantively, the asserted rule is suspect under California law: statutory or regulatory violations can sometimes support negligence per se, but compliance with safety regulations does not generally create automatic insulation from negligence liability.
citation_verdicts.jurisdiction_concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California tort-law standards of care.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I recognize SeaBright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590 (2011), as a California Supreme Court Privette-line decision; the citation details are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: SeaBright held that when a hirer delegates work to an independent contractor, it also presumptively delegates to the contractor any tort-law duty to provide a safe workplace for the contractor's employees, including duties arising from Cal-OSHA or other safety regulations. The injured contractor employee therefore generally could not sue the hirer for breach of those delegated safety duties.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: SeaBright supports the Privette/delegation doctrine, not the proposition that compliance with statutory safety requirements is 'highly probative' of due care or that an OSHA inspection record insulates a hirer from tort liability. The brief appears to convert SeaBright's delegation holding into an evidentiary compliance/due-care rule, which is not the holding I know.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: California Code of Civil Procedure section 335.1 is a real and familiar California statute governing the limitations period for personal injury actions.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Section 335.1 provides a two-year statute of limitations for actions for assault, battery, injury to, or death of an individual caused by the wrongful act or neglect of another.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The statute supports the asserted proposition that California personal-injury claims generally have a two-year limitations period. However, the brief's broader time-bar argument is undermined by its own dates: an injury on March 14, 2021 and filing on March 10, 2023 appears to fall within two years, absent some unusual accrual or tolling issue not established by this citation.
